reasoning: Both models reviewed the only two text files that matter for makepkg execution: PKGBUILD and the .install script. The PKGBUILD fetches a single .cab file from web.archive.org (originally a Microsoft download) with a pinned SHA-256 checksum, then uses cabextract and fontforge to extract and convert fonts into standard system paths. No network access beyond the declared source, no obfuscation, no dangerous operations. The .install script only prints an EULA notice via echo. The binary .cab file was skipped, which is acceptable since checksum-pinned binaries cannot be meaningfully analyzed by LLMs and any tampering would break the hash. This is a textbook benign font package.
learnings: Both models correctly identified that the .cab is checksum-pinned, making binary skip an acceptable risk; the tool could note that a pinned hash provides integrity assurance even when content cannot be inspected.

The 'shallow (2 files reviewed)' trigger is a false alarm for this package — the repo genuinely contains only 3 files (PKGBUILD, .install, .cab), so 2 reviewed + 1 binary skip is complete coverage. The trigger heuristic should account for total file count, not just absolute number reviewed.
Both models gave well-reasoned, accurate explanations distinguishing the harmless source-fetch/parse phase from the package() phase, demonstrating good understanding of makepkg execution semantics.
